text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE

Clinic Date: 28 July 2014

Dr Wang 
Saffron Park Hospital

Saffron Park, London, E14 7JL
Dear Dr Wang
John Doe, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: P546484 NHS No. 5484656746
Flat 15 Roundwood Road, London, E14 7JL

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. He is known to have generalised epilepsy, with a history of generalised tonic–clonic seizures in the past and photosensitivity to flicker and bright light noted on prior assessment. Since his last review, he reports excellent stability and Seizure freedom continues. He attributes this to consistent adherence and careful avoidance of known visual triggers such as strobe lighting, sunlight flicker through trees when travelling, and certain video content. He uses tinted lenses outdoors and has adjusted screen settings on his devices; these measures are patient-reported and appear to have supported stability. ï¿½

He is currently taking Valproate 500 mg b.d. and Levetiracetam (Keppra) 1000 mg b.d. without adverse effects beyond mild intermittent fatigue towards the late afternoon. No mood change, no diplopia, and no new neurological symptoms are reported. Sleep has improved with regular routines. There have been no episodes suggestive of absence, myoclonus, or nocturnal events as per his partner’s account. Ã‚Â·

On examination today he was well, alert, and oriented; cranial nerves were intact; no focal neurological deficits were identified. Blood pressure and pulse were within normal limits. He volunteered that bright supermarket lighting and rapidly changing video content can feel uncomfortable, but there have been no events provoked by these exposures in the past year. Seizure freedom continues, and he is driving within DVLA guidance timeframes as applicable. â€

Given that he has generalised epilepsy with a photosensitive component, we discussed ongoing trigger management. He will continue practical measures for light and flicker exposure, including use of polarising sunglasses in bright conditions and avoidance of strobe environments. No medication changes are planned today in view of sustained remission and tolerability. We agreed routine follow-up in 6–9 months, with earlier review if any breakthrough events, medication side effects, or concerns arise. 

Thank you for your ongoing care. Please let me know if there are any queries. Ã©

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter repeatedly states that the patient is currently seizure free: "Seizure freedom continues." It also notes no episodes suggestive of other seizure types and no events provoked by visual triggers in the past year. However, the only explicit timeframe ("past year") is tied specifically to trigger-provoked events, not to overall seizure freedom. There is no clear, explicit statement that overall seizure freedom has persisted for a full year or for at least six months. Therefore, while the patient is currently seizure free, the duration of overall seizure freedom cannot be confirmed to meet a specific numeric threshold. Per the rules, when seizure-free status is evident but the ≥6-month criterion cannot be firmly confirmed, the appropriate normalization is "seizure free for multiple month."
